# Developing Smart Breeding Methods to Increase Crop Resilience

Utrecht, Netherlands

While today’s crops perform well under stable conditions, future crops must overcome increasingly common and intense heat waves, droughts, floods, nutrient shortages and higher salinity, as well as diseases and pests. As the pace of climate change picks up, speeding the development of resilient crops is urgent. The [Foundation for Food & Agriculture Research](https://foundationfar.org/) (FFAR) is awarding a $5,473,000 grant to [CropXR](https://cropxr.org/), a partnership between several Dutch universities, companies and Dutch granting agencies, to build, support and facilitate a development pipeline for more resilient, sustainable and climate-adapted varieties of agricultural crops. CropXR members provided matching funds for a total $37,438,664 investment.

CropXR’s research, supported by the [Dutch Research Council](https://www.nwo.nl/en)‘s [PlantXR](https://www.nwo.nl/en/researchprogrammes/knowledge-and-innovation-covenant/long-term-programmes-kic-2020-2023/plantxr-a-new-generation-of-intelligent-breeding-tools-for-extra-resilient-crops) research program and the [National Growth Fund](https://www.nationaalgroeifonds.nl/english/the-national-growth-fund), convenes academic groups to integrate multiple disciplines and increase understanding of plant resilience, a complex trait in which many genes and processes interact. Even the most advanced current plant breeding techniques lack the ability to efficiently select for resilience. CropXR’s research is aimed at understanding the mechanisms behind plant resilience and translating that insight into innovative “smart breeding” methods that can enhance complex resilience traits in crops. The research combines plant biology, computational modelling and artificial intelligence to create computational, functional plant models to predict and guide rational, targeted breeding and cultivation strategies.

![Portrait of Angela Records.](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20500'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Portrait of Angela Records.](https://foundationfar.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/05/Angela-Records.png)

Traditional as well as many cutting-edge plant breeding practices have trouble responding quickly to emerging climate threats. This research is integrating a spectrum of technology and knowledge to allow plant breeding to be nimble in meeting our global food and nutritional needs.

In collaboration with leading plant breeding companies, CropXR will also develop a smart breeding method that enables breeders to enhance crop resilience more quickly and efficiently. They will test the feasibility of the new smart breeding method on model crops ranging from lettuce and tomato to potato, onion and ornamental plants.

“This crucial funding from FFAR will not just support basic research and fundamental understanding but also help us translate the new insights into an innovative method for breeding more resilient crops,” said [Dr. Guido Van den Ackerveken](https://www.uu.nl/staff/AFJMvandenAckerveken), CropXR scientific director. “FFAR helps us in our mission to make agriculture worldwide become more sustainable and more climate adaptive.”

CropXR’s smart breeding technology will allow breeders to focus on specific regional crops and stresses, speeding the distribution of resilient crops to growers around the world.

###

## Foundation for Food & Agriculture Research

The [Foundation for Food & Agriculture Research](https://foundationfar.org/) (FFAR) builds public-private partnerships to fund bold research addressing big food and agriculture challenges. FFAR was established in the 2014 Farm Bill to increase public agriculture research investments, fill knowledge gaps and complement the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s research agenda. FFAR’s model matches federal funding from Congress with private funding, delivering a powerful return on taxpayer investment. Through collaboration and partnerships, FFAR advances actionable science benefiting farmers, consumers and the environment.

**Connect:** [@FoundationFAR](https://twitter.com/FoundationFAR?lang=en)

## CropXR

CropXR is an initiative of four Dutch knowledge institutions (Utrecht University, Wageningen University and Research, the University of Amsterdam, and Delft University of Technology) and Plantum, the umbrella organisation of approximately 250 Dutch-based producers of starting materials such as vegetable seeds, seed potatoes and ornamental crops. CropXR is made possible by (1) a major investment provided by the Dutch National Growth Fund and (2) a long-term program, named PlantXR, of the Dutch Research Council (NWO).

CropXR’s mission is to make agricultural production less vulnerable to climate change and less dependent on artificial fertilizers and chemical pesticides.

In addition to knowledge institutions, dozens of public and private partners participate in CropXR’s work, including “green” universities of applied sciences, biotechnology companies, processing industries and large and medium-sized plant breeding companies.

For more information, visit [cropxr.org](http://cropxr.org).

Challenges and prospects of adopting climate-smart agricultural practices and technologies: Implications for food security
==========================================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rusha Begna Wakweya

Add to Mendeley

[https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jafr.2023.100698](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jafr.2023.100698 "Persistent link using digital object identifier")[Get rights and content](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=ELS&contentID=S2666154323002053&orderBeanReset=true)

Under a Creative Commons [license](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/)

Open access

Highlights
----------

*   •Key challenges in CSA adoption includes socioeconomic, political, and institutional. 
*   •Opportunities for adopting CSA were access to resources in different forms. 
*   •Reconsidering the socioeconomic, political, and institutional elements is needed. 
*   •Implementation of multidisciplinary approaches is also required. 

Abstract
--------

Climate-smart [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (CSA) has become popular as a practical solution for food insecurity in the face of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This paper aims to review the challenges and prospects of adopting climate-smart agricultural practices and technologies and their implications for [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in sub-Saharan Africa. The study used an integrative literature review based on inclusion and exclusion criteria. The result of the review indicated that lack of appropriate policies and political commitment, lack of knowledge of CSA technologies by [smallholder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/smallholder "Learn more about smallholder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") farmers, institutional constraints, and financial constraints were identified as the main challenges to the adoption of CSA technologies and practices, while the availability of resources in the form of projects, programs, technologies, practices, and other applications were opportunities for policymakers, advisors, and farmers. Therefore, there is a need for the linkage of farmers, researchers, and extension practitioners, considering appropriate sets of CSA interventions of relevant scale, selected and tailored to local specific contexts and livelihood needs.

Climate-smart agriculture

Prospects

Challenges

Agriculture

Food security

1. Introduction
---------------

[Climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about Climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") has been affected [smallholder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/smallholder "Learn more about smallholder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") farmers in sub-Saharan Africa, threatening their [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and livelihoods [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib1)]. There is an increasing decline in agricultural production, declining food accessibility, and financial success in countries whose livelihood depends on farming due to the increased incidence and severity of the famine, changes in climate, and rain-fed [agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib2)]. Climate change and other structural and institutional challenges have led to a lack of food in Sub-Saharan Africa [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib3)]. Problems in current agricultural development policies include shrinking agricultural frontiers, declining [soil fertility](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/soil-fertility "Learn more about soil fertility from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), feeding a rapidly growing population [[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib4)], and reducing rural poverty [[5](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib5)].

Improving African agriculture requires an integrated approach and a new paradigm to address these interconnected difficulties [[6](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib6)]. Sustainable intensification is a unique path to agricultural transformation in Africa [[7](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib7),[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib8)]. [CSA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-smart-agriculture "Learn more about CSA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a holistic approach that includes context-specific technologies and practices such as sustainable intensification. To address economic and environmental Problems, recent agricultural policies have focused on these techniques [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib10)]. “CSA approaches that increase production and improve adaptability are an option for sustaining agricultural output [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib12)]. Today, agriculture that is climate-smart is presented as an exceptional perspective for meeting both [global warming](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/global-warming "Learn more about global warming from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") reduction and adjustment objectives while also ensuring food security [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib13)].Several developing countries, like Ethiopia, have endorsed climate-smart agriculture technologies and practices. Farmer adoption of CSA is hindered by several challenges [[[13]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib13), [[14]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib14), [[15]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib15)].

CSA is a technique aimed at the improvement of farming methods and the provision of food security in an unstable environment, ignoring the precise context and community-appropriate and flexible outcomes [[[16]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib16), [[17]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib17), [[18]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib18)]. Identifying desirable practices in this environment and developing a method of rewarding officers who follow these habits could be a critical part of CSA. Although this strategy has a lot of potentials, it is difficult to put into practice due to challenging issues and monitoring costs [[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib19)]. According to FAO [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib13)], the novelty of ideas toward the technologies and practices of CSA limited farmer's exposure. Likewise, CSA interventions require a great deal of experience and skill to understand a well-defined site and need substantial growth in the individual's and organization's capabilities in terms of technology, qualified personnel, facilities, and financial resources.

Although it is widely acknowledged that CSA is an important element in the adaptation of agriculture to climate change [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib20),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib21)], studies of the challenges and prospects of CSA adoption on implications for food security of smallholder farmers are lacking.

Furthermore, despite the benefits that CSA technologies promise, the uptake of CSA know-how among African farmers has been facing challenges as re-thinking in CSA increases and the changing aspects of many African cultural, socioeconomic, and environmental countries change [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib22)]. There is a particular need for robust studies that improve understanding of what works where and why in different agro-ecologies and [farming systems](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/farming-system "Learn more about farming systems from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), facilitating what constitutes ‘climate smartness’ in biophysical and socio-economic contexts. Therefore, this paper reviews the challenges and prospects of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security in Sub-Saharan Africa.

2. Materials and methods
------------------------

This review used an integrative literature review method, as per described by Torraco, R.J.[23] and Snyder, H., [24]. Integrative literature review to address the mature or new and emerging topics. In this case, the specific purpose of the review paper always determines the right strategy to use. The review paper identified the challenges and opportunities for adopting [CSA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/agricultural-science "Learn more about CSA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") practices and technologies and the implications of [CSA](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-smart-agriculture "Learn more about CSA from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [food security](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/food-science/food-security "Learn more about food security from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in sub-Saharan African country.

### 2.1. Criteria for inclusion and exclusion

To find relevant information about the challenges and opportunities of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security, the search keywords like ''climate-smart agriculture'' with ''food security'' and ''challenges and prospects'' with ''CSA″ were combined. ENDNOTE reference manager software version 20 was used to compile appropriate works of literature. [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#tbl1) lists the criteria for selecting suitable literature for the study.

Table 1. Criteria for including and excluding documents.

| Selection Procedures | Elimination Standards |
| --- | --- |
| Paper that is written in English | Documents written other than English |
| Documents such as Journal articles, reviews, or chapters of the book | File types that are not journal articles, reviews, as well as book chapters. |
| Studies focused on the farming area | Studies not focused on farming area |
| Talks at slightest single CSA principles (productivity, adaptation, and mitigation) | Reports that exclude CSA principles |
| Documents that contain suitable aspects to carry out data analysis | Papers that do not deliver the necessary elements undertake data examination |

Scopus Source, web of Science, Google Search, Google Scholar data bases and other developmental organization websites like Food and Agricultural Organization (FAO) and Consultative Group on International Agricultural Research (CGIAR) were used. Emphasis has been given to the documents that focus on pillars of CSA in sub-Saharan Africa (See [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#fig1)).

![Image 8: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2666154323002053-gr1.jpg)

3. Result and discussion
------------------------

### 3.1. The implication of CSA for food security

The African agricultural system shows more vulnerability to changing climatic conditions when compared with the other regions [[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib25)]. Specifically, Sub-Saharan Africa is not yet at the peak of [climate change](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/climate-change "Learn more about climate change from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"); yet, past fluctuations in temperature and precipitation have a direct impact on food security and productivity across the continent [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib26)]. Flooding, heat stress, and famine have worsened the already inadequate crop, animal, and [aquatic species](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/aquatic-species "Learn more about aquatic species from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") output and raised rates of starvation and poverty, resulting in a lower quality of life and health [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib27),[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib28)]. SSA will have the highest rates of hunger and poverty compared to other parts of the continent, making it harder to get food because of the rapid population growth [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib29)].

This review mainly focused on the challenges and prospects of adopting CSA practices and technologies and its implication for food security in Sub-Saharan Africa (See [Table 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#tbl2)). The reviewed studies reported that CSA practices and technologies adoption has a [sustainability](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/environmental-impact-assessment "Learn more about sustainability from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") impact on food security (changing plant variety, land rotation, mulching, and crop diversification [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib31)]. Nevertheless, their study lacks in showing the challenges and opportunities of CSA technologies and practices adoption. According to Sarker, Wu [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib32)], in the changing climatic conditions, CSA is an effective approach to enhance agricultural production and feed people across the world. However, the concept of CSA is still evolving, and there is limited research that explores the linkages between CSA and [sustainable agriculture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/sustainable-agriculture "Learn more about sustainable agriculture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and how those practices of CSA can be promoted to achieve food security and development goals.

Table 2. Summary of the findings from the review of the included studies.

| Author/s | Title of the Studies | Country | Major findings on the challenges and prospects of adopting CSA practices and technologies |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Arslan, A. et al. [[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib23)] | Adaptation Implications of the Climate Smart Agriculture | Zambia | Farmer's adaptation to climate change varies by the types of climate impacts and agro-ecology region. |
| Dadzie, S.K.N. et al. [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib24)] | Sustainability responses to climate-smart adaptation in Africa: Implication for food security | Ghana | The sustainability of farm practices is being impacted by the farmers' choice of climate-smart adaptation measures at the farm level. |
| Onyeneke, R.U. et al., [[79](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib79)] | Status of climate-smart agriculture in southeast Nigeria | Nigeria | Education, income, credit, extension, livestock ownership, farming experience, land area cultivated, gender, land ownership, household size, and mass media exposure were determinants factors affecting CSA |
| Andrieu, N. et al. [[68](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib68)] | Prioritizing investments for climate-smart agriculture: Lessons learned from Mali | Mali | The implementation of prioritized practices in research and development programs and the request for support to mainstream CSA by the Ministry of Agriculture and the Parliament. |
| Were, K. et al., [[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib27)] | Smart Strategies for Enhanced Agricultural Resilience and Food Security Under a Changing Climate in Sub-Saharan Africa, in Climate Change and Multi-Dimensional Sustainability in African Agriculture: Climate Change and Sustainability in Agriculture | SSA | No single CSA technology and practice offers a solution to the foregoing challenges and must be integrated when choosing the most suitable climate-smart technologies and practices for any given agro-ecology |
| Partey, S.T. et al. [[28](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib28)] | Developing climate-smart agriculture to face climate variability in West Africa: Challenges and lessons learned. | West Africa | CSA still faces several challenges, including a lack of clear conceptual understanding, and limited enabling policy and financing. The prospects of CSA in West Africa hinge on the capacities of farming households and the region's national institutions to understand the environmental, economic, and social challenges |
| Zougmoré, R.B.et al., [[29](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib29)] | Transforming Food Systems in Africa under Climate Change Pressure: Role of Climate-Smart Agriculture. | Ghana, Senegal, Mali, Burkina Faso, in West Africa, Ethiopia, Kenya, Rwanda, Tanzania and East Africa | CSA can contribute to driving a rapid change in food systems in Africa |
| Mango, N. et al., [[30](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib30)] | Adoption of Small-Scale Irrigation Farming as a CSA Practice and Its Influence on Household Income | Southern Africa | Adoption of small-scale irrigation farming as a climate-smart agriculture practice has a significant positive influence on agricultural income. |

Despite the potentiality of attaining sustainable agricultural development goals by implementing CSA properly, there is a low linkage among the components of CSA at the field level. More importantly, the concept is sometimes poorly understood by various stages stakeholders. A better understanding of the concept of CSA, its linkages with sustainable agriculture, and the pathways to promote CSA will enable a more integrated climate change response and aider adoption of CSA practices towards attaining food security and [sustainable development goals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/sustainable-development-goals "Learn more about sustainable development goals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). As a result, it is critical to reinforce and disseminate CSA approaches at the field level to achieve a more sustainable and prosperous future [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib32)].

Eventhough, there were successful pilot projects, the uptake of new and innovative agricultural technologies and practices has often been poor, and we have still not been able to resolve food insecurity and rural poverty problems. Some studies, including those byWestermann, Förch [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib33)], stated that due to low adoption rates by farmers in developing countries, a variety of agricultural technologies and practices, including some that qualify as CSA, are not having the full impact they could be having. There is a need to show real impact beyond the plot or site level to impact more people over wider areas and on institutions and policies that drive interest in scaling up [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib38)]. Hasan, Desiere [[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib39)], revealed that CSA practices adoption was positively associated with household food security in terms of per capita annual food expenditure. Their study highlighted that increasing the adoption of CSA (especially effective use of [organic fertilizer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/organic-fertilizer "Learn more about organic fertilizer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and mulching) was crucial in enhancing food security through increased [food production](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/food-production "Learn more about food production from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Other researchers (see, for example, [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib40),[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib41)], have found positive effects of CSA practices and technologies on input use efficiency, increased production, farm income, and reducing emerging environmental problems. According to Lipper _et al_. [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib16)], the current evidence base is inadequate to support effective decision-making and sufficiently inaccessible to decision-makers at the national and local levels. Moreover, although there are CSA technologies available in SSA, their utilization is still low. This confirms the report of Totin, and Roncoli [[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib43)], that CSA use by [smallholder](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/smallholder "Learn more about smallholder from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") farms presents an added opportunity to embark on rigorous promotional campaigns for effectiveness.

The African agricultural system shows more vulnerability to changing climatic conditions when compared with the other regions [[44]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib44). Specifically, Sub-Saharan Africa is not yet at the peak of climate change; yet, past fluctuations in temperature and precipitation have a direct impact on food security and productivity across the continent [[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib45)]]. Flood, heat stress, and famine have worsened the already inadequate crop, animal, and aquatic species output and raised rates of starvation and poverty, resulting in a lower quality of life and heal [[[27](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib27)]]. SSA will have the highest rates of hunger and poverty compared to other parts of the continent, making it very difficult to get food because of the rapid population grwh [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2666154323002053#bib40)][]. CSA adoption, and that this could begin with the local media and through the agricultural [extension agents](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/agricultural-and-biological-sciences/extension-agents "Learn more about extension agents from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Capacity building at all levels of CSA discourse is also very important. According to Kombat, Safati [[[46](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/art

enriched_points.expanded_content: Current breeding methods struggle to adapt quickly to climate impacts. Advanced techniques like genomics and AI are needed but are not yet fully effective in enhancing crop resilience.

Navigating complex regulatory frameworks for genetically modified organisms (GMOs) slows down the adoption of innovative breeding practices, hindering timely responses to climate challenges in agriculture.

Skepticism towards genetically modified crops and climate-smart technologies can impede farmer adoption, necessitating educational outreach to improve understanding and acceptance of these innovations.
